questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes mellitus develops severe hypoglycemia and is given a glucagon rescue injection. Glucagon is released from the pancreas, travels through the bloodstream, and acts on distant hepatocytes to raise blood glucose. Which type of cell signaling does this therapy exploit?

explanation.summary: Hormones carried through the blood to act on distant target cells define endocrine signaling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucagon is a peptide hormone secreted into the bloodstream that travels to distant hepatocytes to stimulate glucose release. Signaling that induces a response in cells at a separate, distant location via the blood is by definition endocrine signaling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autocrine signaling occurs when a cell secretes a ligand that binds its own receptors, not distant hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Paracrine signaling acts on nearby local cells by local diffusion, not on distant organs via the blood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Juxtacrine signaling requires direct physical contact between adjacent cells through gap junctions or membrane-bound ligands.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Synaptic signaling releases neurotransmitters across a synaptic cleft between neurons, not hormones into the blood.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with allergic asthma inhales an allergen. Mast cells in the airway wall release histamine, which acts on immediately adjacent smooth muscle and blood vessels to cause local bronchoconstriction and vasodilation. Which mode of cell signaling best describes the action of the released histamine?

explanation.summary: A mediator that diffuses a short distance to act on nearby cells is paracrine signaling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Paracrine signaling propagates a signal to nearby cells. Histamine released from mast cells diffuses locally to act on adjacent smooth muscle and vascular cells, exactly the example the brick gives for paracrine inflammatory mediators.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Endocrine signaling delivers hormones through the bloodstream to distant target cells, not to immediately adjacent tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Autocrine signaling would require histamine to bind receptors on the same mast cell that released it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Synaptic signaling involves neurotransmitter release across a synaptic cleft between neurons.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Juxtacrine signaling requires direct membrane contact or gap junctions rather than a diffusible mediator.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A researcher exposes a person to the nerve agent sarin. Sarin inhibits acetylcholinesterase in the synaptic cleft, preventing hydrolysis of the neurotransmitter and leading to convulsions, paralysis, and cardiac arrest. The normal signaling process disrupted by sarin is an example of which type of cell communication?

explanation.summary: Neurotransmitter release across a synaptic cleft, terminated by acetylcholinesterase, defines synaptic signaling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Synaptic signaling occurs when chemical signals are released from a presynaptic terminal into the synaptic cleft to bind a postsynaptic membrane. Acetylcholinesterase normally terminates the signal; sarin blocks this cessation, causing sustained neurotransmission.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Paracrine signaling uses diffusible mediators acting on nearby cells, not neurotransmitters at a defined synapse.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Endocrine signaling delivers hormones through the blood to distant cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autocrine signaling occurs when a cell responds to a ligand it secreted itself.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Juxtacrine signaling requires direct physical contact between cells rather than a cleft-crossing transmitter.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A steroid hormone derived from cholesterol is administered to a cultured cell. Because of its chemical nature, this hormone readily crosses the plasma membrane and binds a receptor that then regulates gene transcription. Which type of receptor most likely binds this ligand?

explanation.summary: Lipophilic, cholesterol-derived hormones cross the membrane and act through intracellular nuclear receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Steroid hormones are lipophilic and readily cross the lipid bilayer, so they are detected by intracellular nuclear receptors that act as ligand-activated transcription factors, binding DNA to regulate gene expression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Receptor tyrosine kinases are cell-surface receptors for polar ligands such as growth factors that cannot passively cross the membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.B: GPCRs are cell-surface receptors for hydrophilic ligands and signal through second messengers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ligand-gated ion channels sit in the membrane and open in response to polar extracellular ligands.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serine/threonine kinase receptors are membrane enzyme-linked receptors for peptide ligands such as TGF-beta.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A peptide hormone binds its receptor on the surface of a target cell but does not enter the cytoplasm. To generate a cellular response, the cell must produce low-molecular-weight intracellular molecules that propagate and amplify the external signal. What is the fundamental reason a peptide hormone signals in this indirect manner rather than binding an intracellular receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is hydrophilic and too large to passively cross the membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is synthesized directly from cholesterol

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is lipophilic and freely diffuses across the bilayer

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is degraded by phosphodiesterases before entering the cell

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is an iodinated amine that binds nuclear receptors

explanation.summary: Hydrophilic, large peptide hormones cannot cross the membrane, so they use surface receptors and second messengers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Peptide/protein hormones are substantially larger and polar, which along with their hydrophilic nature impedes passive diffusion into the cell. They must bind cell-surface receptors that trigger second messengers to propagate and amplify the signal internally.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cholesterol-derived hormones are steroids, which are lipophilic and use nuclear receptors, unlike peptide hormones.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Peptide hormones are hydrophilic, not lipophilic, and cannot freely diffuse across the bilayer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phosphodiesterases degrade second messengers such as cAMP, not the extracellular peptide hormone itself.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thyroid hormones are iodinated amines that use nuclear receptors, but peptide hormones are a different class.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: Epinephrine binds a beta-adrenergic G protein-coupled receptor. The receptor acts as a guanine nucleotide exchange factor on the associated Galpha subunit, which then dissociates and stimulates adenylate cyclase. Which molecule, when bound to the Galpha subunit, promotes this activation of adenylate cyclase?

explanation.summary: GTP-bound Galpha dissociates from the receptor and Gbeta-gamma to activate adenylate cyclase.
explanation.whyCorrect: On activation, the GPCR catalyzes exchange of GDP for GTP on Galpha. The GTP-bound Galpha subunit dissociates from the beta-gamma subunits and activates adenylate cyclase to generate cAMP, propagating the signal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GDP-bound Galpha is the inactive state formed after intrinsic GTPase hydrolysis shuts the pathway off.
whyWrongByChoice.C: cAMP is the downstream second messenger produced after Galpha activates adenylate cyclase, not the activating nucleotide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ATP is the substrate adenylate cyclase converts into cAMP, not the molecule that binds and activates Galpha.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IP3 is a second messenger of the Gq/phospholipase C pathway, not the nucleotide that activates Galpha-s.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A patient chronically consumes large amounts of caffeine, a methylxanthine that inhibits phosphodiesterase. As a result, the intracellular second messenger that normally activates protein kinase A has a prolonged half-life, sustaining alertness and increased heart rate. Which second messenger accumulates due to this phosphodiesterase inhibition?

explanation.summary: Phosphodiesterases hydrolyze cyclic nucleotides; inhibiting them prolongs cAMP signaling and PKA activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: cAMP is a cyclic purine mononucleotide that activates PKA and is normally destroyed by phosphodiesterases. Caffeine inhibits PDE, prolonging the cAMP half-life to sustain downstream effects such as alertness and increased heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DAG is generated by phospholipase C cleaving PIP2 and activates PKC; it is not degraded by phosphodiesterase.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IP3 is a phospholipase C product that releases calcium and is not a cyclic nucleotide targeted by phosphodiesterase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitric oxide is a gas generated from arginine; it is not a cyclic nucleotide broken down by phosphodiesterase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Calcium ions are released from ER/SR stores and cleared by SERCA pumps, not by phosphodiesterase.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A patient develops profuse watery diarrhea and dehydration after infection with Vibrio cholerae. Cholera toxin covalently modifies a specific Galpha subunit, preventing GTP hydrolysis so the subunit is constitutively active, leading to sustained cAMP production, PKA activation, and chloride efflux into the gut lumen. Which Galpha subunit does cholera toxin lock in the active state?

explanation.summary: Cholera toxin constitutively activates Galpha-s, driving unregulated cAMP production.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholera toxin covalently alters Galpha-s to prevent GTP hydrolysis, keeping it permanently active. The prolonged cAMP and PKA activity opens chloride channels; ion efflux draws water into the gut lumen, causing the severe secretory diarrhea of cholera.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Galpha-i is the target of pertussis toxin, which inhibits an inhibitor of adenylate cyclase, a different mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Galpha-q activates phospholipase C to make IP3 and DAG and is not the cholera toxin target.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Galpha-12/13 activates Rho kinases for cytoskeletal remodeling, unrelated to cholera toxin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Gbeta-gamma complex modulates ion channels such as GIRK; it is not covalently modified by cholera toxin.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: Pertussis toxin from Bordetella pertussis ADP-ribosylates a Galpha subunit, effectively inhibiting an inhibitor of adenylate cyclase. This raises intracellular cAMP and impairs leukocyte function, helping the bacterium evade immune-mediated destruction. Which Galpha subunit is modified by pertussis toxin?

explanation.summary: Pertussis toxin inactivates Galpha-i, removing inhibition of adenylate cyclase and raising cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Galpha-i subtype normally blocks adenylate cyclase activation. Pertussis toxin modifies Galpha-i, inhibiting this inhibitor, which increases cAMP and impairs leukocyte function so the bacterium escapes immune clearance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Galpha-q signals through phospholipase C to produce IP3 and DAG and is not the pertussis toxin target.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Galpha-s stimulates adenylate cyclase directly and is the target of cholera toxin, not pertussis toxin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Galpha-12/13 drives Rho-mediated cytoskeletal remodeling and is unrelated to pertussis toxin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RhoA is a small G-protein downstream of Galpha-12/13, not a heterotrimeric Galpha subunit modified by the toxin.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with obesity has recently developed insulin resistance and early type 2 diabetes. Insulin levels are normal or elevated, but the downstream metabolic signaling cascade is blunted. The insulin receptor is an atypical predimerized receptor tyrosine kinase whose metabolic effects depend on a PI3-kinase pathway. Which parameter is most likely decreased in this patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells

explanation.summary: Blunted insulin signaling reduces downstream PKB/Akt phosphorylation, impairing metabolic responses.
explanation.whyCorrect: Insulin's metabolic branch depends on PIP3-mediated recruitment and PDK1 phosphorylation of PKB/Akt. When insulin signaling is compromised, PKB/Akt activation via phosphorylation falls, reducing GLUT4 translocation and glucose uptake.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The insulin receptor is already predimerized (linked by disulfide bonds), so dimerization is not the limiting step.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin is present or elevated in insulin resistance, so secretion is not decreased at onset.
whyWrongByChoice.D: cAMP is produced from GPCR-mediated signaling; the insulin receptor signals through an RTK pathway instead.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nuclear thyroid receptor activity is unrelated to insulin receptor tyrosine kinase signaling.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: In a research experiment, a ligand is applied to cells expressing a Gq-coupled GPCR, both with and without a SERCA inhibitor. Gq activates phospholipase C, generating IP3 and DAG; IP3 releases intracellular calcium that activates protein kinase C. Compared with cells that have functional SERCA, which parameter is most prolonged in the cells treated with the SERCA inhibitor?

explanation.summary: SERCA normally clears cytosolic calcium; inhibiting it keeps calcium high and prolongs PKC activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gq signaling activates phospholipase C to make IP3 and DAG; IP3 releases calcium that, with DAG, activates PKC. SERCA pumps calcium back into the ER/SR to terminate the signal, so a SERCA inhibitor keeps cytosolic calcium elevated and prolongs PKC activation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: cAMP is produced by the Galpha-s/adenylate cyclase pathway, not by Gq-driven phospholipase C signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PKB/Akt phosphorylation is part of insulin receptor tyrosine kinase signaling, not Gq/calcium signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sustained high calcium would promote calmodulin activation, not inactivation, the opposite of this option.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ROCK is activated downstream of Galpha-12/13 and RhoA, not by Gq-mediated calcium release.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with stable angina places a sublingual nitroglycerine tablet under his tongue during chest pain. Nitroglycerine increases production of a gaseous signaling molecule that diffuses into vascular smooth muscle, activates cytosolic guanylate cyclase to produce cGMP, and causes vasodilation. Which molecule directly mediates this smooth muscle relaxation?

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerine raises nitric oxide, which activates guanylate cyclase to make cGMP and relax vascular smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitric oxide is a gas that freely diffuses across membranes to activate cytosolic guanylate cyclase, producing cGMP that stimulates smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation. This relieves the pain of angina, the mechanism behind sublingual nitroglycerine.

whyWrongByChoice.B: cAMP is generated by adenylate cyclase downstream of Galpha-s and is not the gaseous mediator produced by nitroglycerine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DAG is a membrane-anchored phospholipase C product that activates PKC, not a diffusible vasodilating gas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine is a catecholamine ligand acting on adrenergic GPCRs, not the molecule generated by nitroglycerine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IP3 releases intracellular calcium and is not the gas that activates guanylate cyclase in this pathway.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease and symptomatic anemia is started on epoetin (recombinant erythropoietin) to stimulate red blood cell production. Erythropoietin binds a receptor that lacks intrinsic catalytic activity. Through which of the following mechanisms does this receptor most likely propagate its intracellular signal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intrinsic receptor autophosphorylation on cytoplasmic tyrosines

choices.label: B
choices.text: An associated Janus kinase that phosphorylates STAT proteins

choices.label: C
choices.text: A G-alpha-s subunit that activates adenylate cyclase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Receptor kinase phosphorylation of SMAD transcription factors

choices.label: E
choices.text: IKK-mediated phosphorylation and degradation of IkB

explanation.summary: Cytokine receptors such as the EPO receptor lack intrinsic kinase activity and rely on Janus kinases (JAKs) to phosphorylate STATs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The EPO receptor is a hematopoietic cytokine receptor without catalytic activity of its own. Ligand binding brings associated JAKs into proximity so they phosphorylate the receptor and then STAT proteins, which dimerize and translocate to the nucleus to act as transcription factors driving RBC differentiation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Intrinsic autophosphorylation describes receptor tyrosine kinases (e.g., the EGF receptor), which possess catalytic activity the EPO receptor lacks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: G-alpha-s activation of adenylate cyclase is a GPCR/cAMP mechanism, not the cytokine-receptor pathway used by EPO.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SMAD phosphorylation is characteristic of serine/threonine receptor kinases such as the TGF-beta receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IKK-mediated IkB degradation liberates NF-kB and is downstream of TNF-alpha, not EPO.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A researcher studies how oxygen availability controls erythropoietin (EPO) production. Under well-oxygenated (normoxic) conditions, EPO gene expression is kept low. Which of the following best explains why EPO levels remain low when tissue oxygen is normal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HIF-1-alpha is hydroxylated, bound by VHL, and degraded by the proteasome

choices.label: B
choices.text: HIF-1-alpha dimerizes with HIF-beta and binds the hypoxia response element

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolyl hydroxylases are inactivated, stabilizing HIF-1-alpha

choices.label: D
choices.text: JAK2 constitutively phosphorylates STAT independent of ligand

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gene amplification increases the number of EPO gene copies

explanation.summary: In normoxia, oxygen-dependent prolyl hydroxylases mark HIF-1-alpha for VHL-mediated ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, keeping EPO low.
explanation.whyCorrect: When oxygen is abundant, prolyl hydroxylases (PHDs) hydroxylate HIF-1-alpha. Hydroxylated HIF-1-alpha is recognized by the von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) protein, which promotes ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, preventing transcription of hypoxia genes like EPO.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Dimerization of HIF-1-alpha with HIF-beta at the hypoxia response element occurs during hypoxia to raise EPO, not in normoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PHDs are oxygen-dependent and active in normoxia; their inactivation stabilizes HIF-1-alpha and occurs during hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Constitutive JAK2 signaling describes polycythemia vera, not the normal oxygen-sensing regulation of EPO.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gene amplification is an overexpression mechanism seen in cancers such as HER2+ breast cancer, not physiologic EPO regulation.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man reports intense itching after hot showers, facial redness, and headaches. Laboratory studies show an elevated red blood cell count, and genetic testing identifies a JAK2 V617F mutation. This mutation causes constitutive signaling that mimics the binding of which of the following?

explanation.summary: Polycythemia vera results from a JAK2 mutation that constitutively activates the JAK-STAT pathway normally triggered by erythropoietin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The clinical picture—elevated RBCs, aquagenic pruritus, plethora—is polycythemia vera, driven by JAK2 V617F. Because JAK2 relays erythropoietin signaling to promote RBC production, constitutive JAK2 activity mimics ongoing EPO stimulation even without ligand.

whyWrongByChoice.A: TNF-alpha activates the NF-kB inflammatory cascade, not the erythropoietic JAK2/STAT pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibroblast growth factor acts through FGFR-3 in bone growth (achondroplasia), unrelated to RBC overproduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alpha-MSH signals through MC1R/cAMP for melanin production, not JAK2-driven erythropoiesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TGF-beta signals through SMAD proteins and is not the ligand relayed by JAK2 in erythropoiesis.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man presents with fatigue, splenomegaly, and a markedly elevated white blood cell count. Cytogenetics reveal a t(9;22) translocation. The resulting fusion gene produces a protein that drives his disease. Which of the following best describes the pathologic protein?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A constitutively active nonreceptor cytosolic tyrosine kinase

choices.label: D
choices.text: A transcription factor with a phosphomimetic substitution

choices.label: E
choices.text: A soluble decoy receptor lacking intracellular signaling

explanation.summary: The Philadelphia chromosome fuses BCR and ABL, yielding a constitutively active nonreceptor tyrosine kinase that drives chronic myeloid leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The t(9;22) translocation joins the ABL gene—encoding a freely diffusible cytosolic (nonreceptor) tyrosine kinase—to BCR. The BCR-ABL fusion retains full kinase activity but loses regulatory sites, causing constitutive pro-growth signaling and CML.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ABL is explicitly not a receptor tyrosine kinase; it is a cytoplasmic protein, and the defect is a fusion, not overexpression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A constitutively active G-alpha-s subunit characterizes McCune-Albright syndrome, not CML.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The BCR-ABL defect is a gross structural fusion, not a single phosphomimetic amino-acid substitution in a transcription factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A soluble decoy receptor describes etanercept, a therapeutic, not the leukemogenic fusion protein.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A cancer biologist studies a colon epithelial cell in which Wnt binds the Frizzled receptor, activating the intracellular component Dishevelled. Which of the following is the most direct downstream consequence of Dishevelled activation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of the Axin/APC/GSK-3-beta destruction complex, stabilizing beta-catenin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autophosphorylation of Frizzled on cytoplasmic tyrosine residues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phosphorylation of STAT proteins by Janus kinases

choices.label: D
choices.text: Degradation of IkB to release NF-kB into the nucleus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased transbinding of E-cadherin between adjacent cells

explanation.summary: In Wnt signaling, Dishevelled inhibits the beta-catenin destruction complex, allowing beta-catenin to accumulate and enter the nucleus.
explanation.whyCorrect: When Wnt binds Frizzled (a GPCR), Dishevelled is activated and inhibits the complex of Axin, APC, GSK-3-beta, and CKI-alpha that normally targets beta-catenin for degradation. By 'inhibiting the inhibitor,' free beta-catenin translocates to the nucleus to promote gene expression.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Frizzled is a GPCR without intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity, so it does not autophosphorylate like an RTK.
whyWrongByChoice.C: STAT phosphorylation by JAKs is part of the cytokine JAK-STAT pathway, not Wnt/Frizzled signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IkB degradation releasing NF-kB is the TNF-alpha inflammatory cascade, unrelated to Dishevelled.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Wnt signaling frees beta-catenin for migration-promoting transcription; increased E-cadherin transbinding instead sequesters beta-catenin at the membrane.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with early pubic hair, a growth spurt placing him at the 97th height percentile, enlarged testicles, and hyperpigmented macules on his torso. Imaging shows craniofacial fibrous dysplasia. Genetic testing points to a somatic mutation in a cell-signaling gene. Which of the following is the most likely pathophysiologic mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constitutive G-alpha-s activation causing excessive adenylate cyclase and cAMP

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activating mutation of fibroblast growth factor receptor-3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expression of a BCR-ABL fusion protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amplification of a receptor tyrosine kinase gene

explanation.summary: McCune-Albright syndrome arises from a somatic GNAS mutation that constitutively activates G-alpha-s, overstimulating adenylate cyclase and cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The triad of precocious puberty, cafe-au-lait spots, and fibrous dysplasia is McCune-Albright syndrome. A somatic (non-inherited) mutation in GNAS locks G-alpha-s in the active state, overproducing cAMP and driving excessive hormone synthesis and abnormal bone and pigment cell behavior.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An activating FGFR-3 mutation causes achondroplasia (short-limbed dwarfism), not precocious puberty with fibrous dysplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A BCR-ABL fusion causes leukemia through an abnormal tyrosine kinase, not this endocrine/skeletal picture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loss of EPO expression would cause anemia and does not match these findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RTK gene amplification drives cancers such as HER2+ breast cancer, not McCune-Albright syndrome.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy has disproportionately short upper arms and thighs, a long narrow trunk, macrocephaly with frontal bossing, and normal cognitive development. The condition results from an activating mutation in a receptor tyrosine kinase. Which of the following best explains the short stature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhanced chondrocyte proliferation accelerating endochondral ossification

choices.label: B
choices.text: FGFR-3 signaling that suppresses chondrocyte proliferation and cartilage formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deficient intramembranous ossification of the skull and ribs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constitutive JAK2 activation impairing bone marrow output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss-of-function of G-alpha-s reducing cAMP in osteoblasts

explanation.summary: Achondroplasia is caused by an activating FGFR-3 mutation that suppresses chondrocyte proliferation, limiting the cartilage-based (endochondral) growth of long bones.
explanation.whyCorrect: In achondroplasia, gain-of-function FGFR-3 autophosphorylates without ligand. FGFR-3 signaling suppresses chondrocyte differentiation and proliferation, decreasing cartilage formation needed for endochondral ossification of the long bones, producing rhizomelic shortening with preserved intelligence.

whyWrongByChoice.A: FGFR-3 activation suppresses—rather than enhances—chondrocyte proliferation, so bone growth is inhibited.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Flat bones like the skull form by intramembranous ossification, which is relatively preserved; the defect impairs endochondral ossification of long bones.
whyWrongByChoice.D: JAK2 activation drives polycythemia vera, not the skeletal phenotype of achondroplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Achondroplasia is a gain-of-function RTK mutation, not a loss of G-alpha-s/cAMP signaling.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A pathologist evaluates a breast tumor that is classified as HER2-positive. HER2 is a pro-growth receptor tyrosine kinase. Which of the following mechanisms most commonly accounts for the increased HER2 signaling in this cancer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A phosphomimetic substitution stabilizing the active receptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gene amplification producing extra HER2 gene copies

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chromosomal translocation creating a fusion protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Altered epigenetic imprinting silencing the receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: A promoter mutation that decreases transcription

explanation.summary: HER2-positive breast cancer results from amplification/duplication of the HER2 gene, overexpressing a pro-growth RTK.
explanation.whyCorrect: Overexpression can amplify a pro-growth pathway, and in HER2+ breast cancer the receptor is overproduced due to an increased number of HER2 gene copies (gene amplification/duplication). This excessive receptor density drives uncontrolled proliferative signaling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A phosphomimetic substitution is an activating point mutation; HER2+ cancer reflects overexpression from extra gene copies, not a mimic of phosphorylation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A translocation-derived fusion protein describes BCR-ABL in leukemia, not HER2 amplification.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epigenetic silencing would reduce, not increase, receptor levels, opposite to the overexpression seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A promoter mutation that decreases transcription would lower HER2, whereas the disease is defined by overexpression.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: In a macrophage, TNF-alpha binds TNFR-1 and triggers the NF-kB cascade. The NF-kB complex is normally held inactive in the cytoplasm. Which of the following events most directly liberates NF-kB so it can translocate to the nucleus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dimerization and nuclear translocation of STAT proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: IKK-mediated phosphorylation of IkB, marking it for proteasomal degradation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolyl hydroxylation of NF-kB by oxygen-dependent enzymes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphorylation of SMAD proteins by an activated receptor kinase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of the Axin/APC/GSK-3-beta destruction complex

explanation.summary: NF-kB is sequestered by IkB; IKK phosphorylates IkB, targeting it for ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, freeing NF-kB.
explanation.whyCorrect: The inhibitor of kappa-B (IkB) keeps NF-kB in the cytoplasm. Upon receptor activation, the IkB kinase (IKK) complex phosphorylates IkB, a post-translational mark that leads to its ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, releasing NF-kB (p50/p65) to enter the nucleus and induce gene expression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: STAT dimerization is the JAK-STAT pathway, not the mechanism releasing NF-kB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prolyl hydroxylation regulates HIF-1-alpha in oxygen sensing, not NF-kB liberation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SMAD phosphorylation belongs to TGF-beta serine/threonine receptor signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inhibiting the destruction complex frees beta-catenin in Wnt signaling, not NF-kB.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis is prescribed etanercept, a recombinant protein resembling the shed, proteolytically cleaved TNFR-1. Which of the following best describes how etanercept reduces inflammation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a monoclonal antibody that opsonizes synovial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: It acts as a soluble decoy that binds TNF-alpha but cannot transduce a signal

choices.label: C
choices.text: It phosphorylates IkB to prevent NF-kB release

choices.label: D
choices.text: It blocks the ATP-binding pocket of intracellular tyrosine kinases

choices.label: E
choices.text: It increases membrane-bound TNFR-1 to enhance signaling

explanation.summary: Etanercept is a soluble decoy receptor that intercepts TNF-alpha before it engages membrane receptors, dampening NF-kB-driven inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Etanercept mimics the shed soluble TNFR-1. Because it is not membrane-bound, it binds circulating TNF-alpha but cannot transduce an intracellular signal, preventing the cytokine from engaging cell-surface receptors and thereby reducing immune-related gene expression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Etanercept is a recombinant decoy receptor, not a monoclonal antibody (that description fits adalimumab).
whyWrongByChoice.C: It sequesters extracellular TNF-alpha; it does not act intracellularly to phosphorylate IkB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Blocking a kinase ATP pocket describes small-molecule inhibitors like imatinib, not etanercept.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Etanercept intercepts TNF-alpha to reduce signaling; it does not increase membrane TNFR-1.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A neonate has delayed separation of the umbilical cord, recurrent skin and mucosal infections, and notably absent pus formation at sites of infection. The condition is caused by a defect in an adhesion molecule required for neutrophils to exit blood vessels. Which of the following molecules is most likely defective?

explanation.summary: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency results from a defect in the LFA-1 integrin (CD18), impairing neutrophil adhesion and transmigration.
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic triad—delayed umbilical cord separation, recurrent infections, and absent pus—is leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD). It stems from a defect in the LFA-1 integrin (CD18), which mediates firm adhesion of leukocytes to vascular endothelium; without it, neutrophils cannot transmigrate to sites of infection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: E-cadherin mediates epithelial cell-cell adhesion and beta-catenin signaling, not neutrophil transmigration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: P-selectin mediates initial leukocyte rolling, but LAD is specifically due to an integrin (CD18) defect impairing firm adhesion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-catenin is an intracellular transcription factor in Wnt/cadherin signaling, not a neutrophil adhesion receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Frizzled is the Wnt GPCR, unrelated to leukocyte adhesion and transmigration.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A researcher observes that as an epithelial tumor becomes more migratory, its cells internalize E-cadherin. Which of the following best describes the downstream signaling consequence that promotes this migratory, mesenchymal phenotype?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-catenin is released and translocates to the nucleus to alter gene expression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-catenin is further sequestered at the plasma membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: STAT proteins are dephosphorylated and retained in the cytoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: IkB is stabilized, blocking NF-kB activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: HIF-1-alpha is hydroxylated and degraded by the proteasome

explanation.summary: Loss of E-cadherin releases beta-catenin, which enters the nucleus and reprograms gene expression to support the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cadherin receptors normally sequester beta-catenin at the membrane. When a cell becomes migratory and internalizes E-cadherin, beta-catenin is freed, translocates to the nucleus, binds transcription factors, and shifts the gene expression profile toward movement and migration, facilitating the EMT seen in cancer progression.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Internalizing E-cadherin releases beta-catenin from the membrane rather than sequestering more of it there.
whyWrongByChoice.C: STAT phosphorylation status is part of JAK-STAT signaling and is not the mechanism of cadherin-driven EMT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IkB stabilization concerns NF-kB inflammatory signaling, not E-cadherin/beta-catenin behavior.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HIF-1-alpha degradation is the oxygen-sensing pathway and is unrelated to E-cadherin internalization.

questions.topicName: Overview of Cell Signaling (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man has plasma glucose measured after an overnight fast. Given that the normal fasting range is 70-100 mg/dL, which of the following measured values is most indicative of hypoglycemia?

explanation.summary: Fasting plasma glucose below the normal 70-100 mg/dL range indicates hypoglycemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: After an overnight (basal, post-absorptive) fast the normal plasma glucose range is 70-100 mg/dL. A value of 50 mg/dL falls below this range and represents hypoglycemia, a state that first manifests with CNS symptoms because the brain depends on circulating glucose.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 72 mg/dL sits within the normal fasting range of 70-100 mg/dL.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 90 mg/dL is a normal fasting glucose value.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 110 mg/dL is elevated for a fast (pre-diabetic range 101-125), not low.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 140 mg/dL is hyperglycemic for a fasting individual, not hypoglycemic.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A physiologist notes that skeletal muscle stores large amounts of glycogen, yet mobilizing this glycogen during a fast does not raise blood glucose. Which of the following best explains why muscle glycogen cannot contribute to circulating glucose?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Skeletal muscle converts all glycogen directly to lactate before it can be released

explanation.summary: Only the liver expresses glucose 6-phosphatase, so only hepatic glycogen can supply free blood glucose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucose 6-phosphatase converts glucose 6-phosphate to free glucose, and GLUTs cannot transport phosphorylated glucose. The liver expresses this enzyme so its glycogen-derived glucose exits via GLUT2, whereas skeletal muscle lacks glucose 6-phosphatase and therefore consumes its glycogen locally for catabolism.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Skeletal muscle contains active glycogen phosphorylase and does break down its own glycogen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Muscle synthesizes glycogen normally via glycogen synthase; storage is not the problem.
whyWrongByChoice.D: GLUT2 is the hepatic transporter; muscle uses GLUT4, but the export block is due to missing glucose 6-phosphatase, not the transporter type.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscle glycogen is mobilized for local catabolism, not obligatorily converted entirely to lactate for release.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant is brought in after beginning to sleep through the night. During these longer fasts the infant develops severe hypoglycemia with pronounced lactic acidosis, and examination reveals hepatomegaly. Symptoms improve with frequent feedings. A deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Von Gierke disease (glycogen storage disease type I) is a glucose 6-phosphatase deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Von Gierke disease, glycogen is formed and degraded normally, but liberated glucose is trapped as glucose 6-phosphate because glucose 6-phosphatase is deficient, causing hepatomegaly. Because the liver cannot release glucose, fasting triggers severe hypoglycemia with lactic acidosis, and treatment is frequent meals to prevent the fasting state.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glycogen phosphorylase deficiency impairs glycogen breakdown, but the brick specifically attributes this trapped-glucose, hepatomegaly, lactic-acidosis picture to glucose 6-phosphatase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glycerol kinase is a hepatic enzyme for glycerol handling and does not produce this glycogen-storage picture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hormone-sensitive lipase acts on adipocyte triglycerides, unrelated to hepatic glycogen and lactic acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucokinase phosphorylates glucose entering the liver and would not cause glycogen accumulation with hepatomegaly.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: During a fast, glucagon stimulates hepatic glycogen breakdown but has essentially no effect on skeletal muscle glycogen. Which of the following best explains this tissue selectivity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Skeletal muscle lacks the glucagon receptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skeletal muscle cannot generate cAMP by any mechanism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Skeletal muscle contains no glycogen to mobilize

choices.label: E
choices.text: Skeletal muscle lacks protein kinase A

explanation.summary: Skeletal muscle does not express the glucagon GPCR, so glucagon cannot act on muscle glycogen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucagon signals through a GPCR that is present on hepatocytes but absent on skeletal muscle. Because muscle lacks the glucagon receptor, fasting-associated glucagon does not alter muscle glycogen stores; muscle glycogen is instead mobilized by epinephrine, AMP, and calcium.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Muscle possesses adenylate cyclase, which is engaged by epinephrine acting through adrenergic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Muscle readily generates cAMP downstream of epinephrine; the block is the missing glucagon receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Skeletal muscle holds the bulk of the body's glycogen; it simply is not responsive to glucagon.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscle expresses PKA and uses it in epinephrine signaling.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: During prolonged fasting, hormone-sensitive lipase in adipocytes liberates glycerol and fatty acids. The glycerol is released into the blood for hepatic use rather than being reincorporated locally. Adipocytes release glycerol because they lack which of the following enzymes?

explanation.summary: Adipocytes lack glycerol kinase, so glycerol from lipolysis cannot be phosphorylated and instead exits the cell.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glycerol kinase reversibly forms glycerol 3-phosphate from glycerol and is expressed in the liver, not adipocytes. Because adipocytes lack glycerol kinase, the glycerol released by lipolysis cannot be recaptured and is exported to the liver, which uses it as a gluconeogenic substrate.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Adipocytes do have glycerol 3-phosphate dehydrogenase, which lets them make glycerol 3-phosphate from DHAP when glucose is abundant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hormone-sensitive lipase is present and is precisely the enzyme carrying out the lipolysis described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lipoprotein lipase acts on circulating VLDL/chylomicron triglycerides outside the cell and is not the reason glycerol is released.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucose 6-phosphatase is a hepatic enzyme of glucose metabolism, unrelated to adipocyte glycerol handling.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A healthy 30-year-old eats a carbohydrate-rich meal, and glucose uptake into skeletal muscle and adipose tissue rapidly increases. This increase results from insulin-stimulated deposition of which of the following glucose transporters at the cell surface?

explanation.summary: Insulin promotes translocation of high-affinity GLUT4 to the surface of muscle and adipose cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Insulin promotes deposition of pre-formed GLUT4 transporters onto the surface of adipose and skeletal muscle. GLUT4 has a low Km (high affinity) for glucose, so its appearance causes rapid glucose uptake that fuels oxidation, glycogen synthesis, and lipogenesis in the fed state.

whyWrongByChoice.B: GLUT2 is the hepatic (and pancreatic) transporter that exports glucose and is not insulin-recruited to muscle and fat.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GLUT1 provides basal, insulin-independent glucose uptake and is not the transporter mobilized by insulin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SGLT1 is a sodium-coupled intestinal/renal glucose transporter, unrelated to insulin-stimulated uptake in muscle and fat.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GLUT5 primarily transports fructose and is not responsive to insulin in this manner.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A teenager eats a burger, fries, and a milkshake, then sleeps for 13 hours. Compared with the immediate post-meal state, which of the following changes is most expected upon awakening?

explanation.summary: An overnight fast lowers insulin and raises glucagon, activating adipocyte hormone-sensitive lipase.
explanation.whyCorrect: During the overnight fast, insulin falls and glucagon rises (a low insulin:glucagon ratio). Glucagon activates PKA via cAMP, phosphorylating and activating hormone-sensitive lipase to liberate fatty acids and glycerol from adipocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The insulin-to-glucagon ratio falls, not rises, during a fast.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chylomicrons depend on recently ingested dietary fat and decrease after an overnight fast.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucagon favors the phosphatase activity of PFK-2, dampening its kinase activity and slowing hepatic glycolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucagon-driven PKA phosphorylates and inactivates glycogen synthase, decreasing its activity.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A man eats a candy bar and, moments later, is startled by an assailant and must flee. Despite the recent high-carbohydrate intake, his blood glucose remains elevated to fuel skeletal muscle. Which hormone is most responsible for overriding insulin signaling in this scenario?

explanation.summary: Epinephrine mobilizes fuel during acute stress independent of blood glucose or insulin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epinephrine's metabolic effects mirror glucagon's but occur independent of blood glucose levels. During fight-or-flight, epinephrine overrides insulin signaling to keep glucose elevated, producing cAMP to drive glycogenolysis and hormone-sensitive lipase activity so fuel is available for muscle.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glucagon is released when glucose is low; after a candy bar, glucose is high and glucagon secretion would not be driving this response.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Insulin promotes fuel storage and would lower glucose, the opposite of what is observed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cortisol supports prolonged stress through gene expression over hours, not the immediate override described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Growth hormone is not the acute mediator emphasized here; epinephrine drives the immediate fight-or-flight response.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: During a sprint, a nerve impulse triggers glycogen breakdown in skeletal muscle even without a rise in circulating hormones. Which of the following mechanisms most directly links the nerve impulse to activation of glycogen phosphorylase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calcium binds calmodulin, which activates phosphorylase kinase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucagon activates adenylate cyclase in the myocyte

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cortisol induces transcription of glycogen phosphorylase

explanation.summary: Nerve impulses release Ca2+, and Ca2+-calmodulin activates phosphorylase kinase to turn on glycogen phosphorylase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Muscle nerve conduction prompts Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. This second messenger activates calmodulin, which binds phosphorylase kinase and promotes its activity toward glycogen phosphorylase, driving glycogenolysis for contracting muscle.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Skeletal muscle lacks the glucagon receptor, so glucagon does not act on the myocyte.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Insulin/GLUT4 signaling promotes glucose uptake and storage, not glycogen breakdown.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dephosphorylation inactivates glycogen phosphorylase, opposing the mobilization that occurs during a sprint.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cortisol acts over hours via gene expression and does not provide the immediate contraction-linked activation.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A patient has not eaten for several days and is in the prolonged fasting (starvation) state. Which of the following fuels does the brain rely on most heavily under these conditions?

explanation.summary: In prolonged fasting the brain shifts from glucose to ketone bodies for fuel.
explanation.whyCorrect: The organ fuel summary shows the brain uses glucose in the well-fed and fasting states but shifts to ketone bodies during prolonged fasting. This switch takes several days to develop and spares glucose for tissues that remain glucose-dependent.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Free fatty acids do not readily cross the blood-brain barrier and are not the brain's fuel in starvation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose fuels the brain in the well-fed and short-term fasting states, but is largely replaced by ketones in prolonged fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amino acids are mobilized for gluconeogenesis in the liver, not oxidized directly by the brain as its main fuel.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycerol serves as a hepatic gluconeogenic substrate, not a direct brain fuel.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: In the liver during a fast, glucagon activates protein kinase A, which phosphorylates glycogen synthase. What is the effect of this phosphorylation on glycogen synthase activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It inactivates glycogen synthase, inhibiting glycogen synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: It activates glycogen synthase, increasing glycogen synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It activates glycogen synthase only in skeletal muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: It converts glycogen synthase into phosphorylase kinase

choices.label: E
choices.text: It has no effect because glycogen synthase is not a PKA target

explanation.summary: Glucagon-driven PKA phosphorylation inactivates glycogen synthase, halting glycogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucagon works through cAMP and PKA to phosphorylate glycogen synthase, which inactivates it and inhibits glycogen synthesis. This complements PKA phosphorylation of phosphorylase kinase and glycogen phosphorylase, which together promote glycogen degradation during fasting.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phosphorylation of glycogen synthase inactivates the anabolic enzyme; insulin (via dephosphorylation) is what activates it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The inactivating effect is not restricted to skeletal muscle, and the liver is the site described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phosphorylation modifies glycogen synthase's activity; it does not transform it into a different enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycogen synthase is a genuine PKA target, and its phosphorylation is a key step in switching off glycogenesis.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A researcher compares fuel use across metabolic states. Which of the following cell types relies on glucose across the well-fed, fasting, AND prolonged fasting states without ever switching to an alternative fuel?

explanation.summary: Red blood cells use glucose in every metabolic state, including prolonged fasting.
explanation.whyCorrect: The organ fuel table lists red blood cells as using glucose in the well-fed, fasting, and prolonged fasting states. Lacking mitochondria, RBCs cannot oxidize fatty acids or ketone bodies and remain obligately glucose-dependent throughout.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac muscle uses fatty acids when well-fed and shifts to fatty acids plus ketone bodies during fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The liver uses predominately glucose when well-fed but switches to fatty acids during fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adipocytes use glucose when well-fed but rely on fatty acids during fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The brain uses glucose when well-fed and fasting but shifts to ketone bodies in prolonged fasting.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State

questions.stem: A researcher studies isolated pancreatic beta-cells. When extracellular glucose is raised, the cells depolarize and secrete insulin. Which of the following events most directly links glucose metabolism to membrane depolarization in these cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rising ATP closes ATP-sensitive potassium (K_ATP) channels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rising ATP opens voltage-gated calcium channels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glucose binds and directly opens calcium channels

choices.label: D
choices.text: GLUT2 actively pumps potassium out of the cell

choices.label: E
choices.text: Secreted insulin binds receptors on the same beta-cell

explanation.summary: Glucose-derived ATP closes K_ATP channels, which depolarizes the beta-cell membrane.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucose entering the beta-cell is oxidatively catabolized to generate ATP. The increased ATP acts as an allosteric inhibitor of the ATP-sensitive potassium channel, blocking potassium leak. Trapped potassium reduces the charge differential across the membrane, causing depolarization that then opens voltage-gated calcium channels.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Calcium channels are voltage-gated and open in response to the depolarization that follows K_ATP closure, not directly in response to ATP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose does not directly gate calcium channels; it acts through metabolism to ATP and channel closure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: GLUT2 is a facilitative glucose transporter, not a potassium pump.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Insulin binding to peripheral receptors drives glucose uptake in muscle and fat; it is not the depolarizing trigger within the beta-cell.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is started on a sulfonylurea to lower his blood glucose. The drug acts on pancreatic beta-cells. Which of the following best describes how it augments insulin secretion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It blocks ATP-sensitive potassium channels, promoting depolarization

choices.label: B
choices.text: It opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels to hyperpolarize the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: It activates GLUT2 transporters to increase glucose entry

choices.label: D
choices.text: It stimulates insulin exocytosis independent of calcium

explanation.summary: Sulfonylureas close the K_ATP channel pharmacologically, bypassing the need for glucose-derived ATP.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sulfonylureas inhibit the beta-cell ATP-sensitive potassium channel independent of glucose metabolism. Closing the channel depolarizes the membrane, opens voltage-gated calcium channels, and drives insulin exocytosis. Because the strategy amplifies endogenous secretion, the patient's beta-cells must still be able to produce insulin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Opening the channel would hyperpolarize the cell and suppress secretion, the opposite of the drug's action.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sulfonylureas act on the potassium channel, not on GLUT2 glucose transport.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Secretion still depends on the downstream depolarization-triggered calcium influx.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking calcium channels would prevent, not promote, insulin release.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: Pancreatic beta-cells rely on GLUT2, a transporter with a relatively high Km (low affinity) but high Vmax for glucose. What is the physiological advantage of this transporter property for insulin secretion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Substantial glucose enters the beta-cell only when blood glucose is elevated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucose is transported into the cell against its concentration gradient

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-cells continue to take up glucose avidly during hypoglycemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucose uptake by the beta-cell becomes insulin-dependent

choices.label: E
choices.text: Glucose entering the cell is prevented from being oxidized

explanation.summary: GLUT2's low affinity makes beta-cell glucose uptake proportional to blood glucose, so insulin is released only when glucose is high.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because GLUT2 has low affinity, glucose flows into the beta-cell in meaningful amounts only when circulating glucose concentrations are elevated. This ensures the ATP-driven secretion cascade fires when it is needed. Having insulin present during hypoglycemia would be dangerous, driving glucose even lower and depriving the CNS.

whyWrongByChoice.B: GLUT2 is a facilitative transporter that moves glucose down its gradient, not against it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The point of low affinity is precisely that uptake is minimal during hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-cell uptake through GLUT2 is insulin-independent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Imported glucose is oxidatively catabolized to make the ATP that triggers secretion.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman has recurrent episodes of confusion relieved by eating. During an episode she is hypoglycemic with inappropriately elevated serum insulin. To distinguish endogenous insulin overproduction (such as an insulinoma) from injection of recombinant insulin, which laboratory value is most useful?

explanation.summary: C-peptide is co-secreted equimolar with endogenous insulin but is absent from recombinant insulin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endogenous insulin is packaged with C-peptide in equimolar amounts, so an insulinoma raises both insulin and C-peptide. Recombinant injected insulin lacks C-peptide entirely. Therefore a high C-peptide points to endogenous overproduction, whereas a low C-peptide with high insulin indicates an exogenous source.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blood glucose is low in both scenarios and cannot separate the two causes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Insulin excess does not produce an anion-gap acidosis, so this is unhelpful here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Insulin shock does not cause hepatocellular injury, so transaminases are not discriminating.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HbA1c reflects chronic average glycemia, not the acute source of excess insulin.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: Within the pancreatic beta-cell, proinsulin is converted into the mature, active hormone before storage. Conversion of proinsulin to mature insulin requires which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proteolytic cleavage and release of the C-peptide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Removal of the N-terminal signal peptide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Self-assembly of the protein into a hexamer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Formation of a disulfide bond between the A and C chains

explanation.summary: Mature insulin is generated when the C-peptide is cleaved out of proinsulin, leaving the disulfide-linked A and B chains.
explanation.whyCorrect: Proinsulin already has its signal peptide removed; the final maturation step is proteolytic cleavage that releases the connecting C-peptide. This leaves the A and B chains as a disulfide-linked heterodimer, the active hormone. C-peptide and insulin are then stored equimolar in granules.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Signal peptide removal occurs earlier, converting preproinsulin to proinsulin, not proinsulin to insulin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hexamer formation is a storage/aggregation phenomenon, not the maturation cleavage step.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mature insulin's disulfide bonds link the A and B chains; the C chain is removed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Insulin maturation is driven by proteolysis, not glycosylation.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A pharmaceutical company designs a rapid-acting insulin analog by mutating the primary sequence to prevent self-association after subcutaneous injection. This modification speeds the onset of action for which reason?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The monomer is the most active form, so preventing hexamers speeds availability

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hexamer is the receptor-binding active form

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monomeric insulin cannot bind the insulin receptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: The analog retains extra C-peptide to speed activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mutation adds disulfide bonds that stabilize the active hexamer

explanation.summary: Insulin is active as a monomer; hexamers are inactive, so blocking oligomerization gives faster action.
explanation.whyCorrect: At high concentration insulin self-associates into inactive hexamers that cannot bind the receptor. Dilution normally dissociates these into active monomers. A fast-acting analog is engineered to resist oligomerization so active monomers are available sooner, while long-acting formulations deliberately favor oligomers to slow release.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The hexamer is the inactive storage form, not the receptor-binding species.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The monomer is precisely the form that binds and activates the receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Purified injectable insulins lack C-peptide entirely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The engineering prevents oligomerization rather than stabilizing hexamers.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A sedentary patient with type 2 diabetes is advised to exercise using large muscle groups such as the quadriceps and glutes. Exercising skeletal muscle increases glucose uptake even in the absence of insulin. This insulin-independent uptake occurs because muscle contraction does which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activates AMPK, which promotes GLUT4 translocation to the cell surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stimulates insulin secretion directly from skeletal muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases GLUT2 expression on the muscle membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directly activates the insulin receptor tyrosine kinase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibits AMPK to conserve ATP during activity

explanation.summary: Muscle contraction consumes ATP and raises AMP, activating AMPK, which drives GLUT4 to the membrane without insulin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Contraction expends ATP and raises intracellular AMP, activating AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK). AMPK promotes trafficking of pre-formed GLUT4 vesicles to the plasma membrane, so glucose uptake increases independent of insulin signaling. This is why exercise engaging large muscle masses aids glucose clearance in diabetes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin is secreted by pancreatic beta-cells, not by skeletal muscle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Muscle uses GLUT4, not GLUT2, and the effect is on trafficking rather than GLUT2 expression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pathway bypasses the insulin receptor entirely, which is the defining feature of insulin-independent uptake.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Exercise activates AMPK; inhibiting it would not promote glucose uptake.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: During intense exercise, AMPK is strongly activated in skeletal muscle to restore energy balance. Which of the following processes is suppressed as a direct consequence of AMPK activation?

explanation.summary: AMPK inhibits ATP-consuming anabolic pathways such as glycogenesis while enhancing ATP-producing catabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: AMPK signals a low-energy state and therefore inhibits anabolic, ATP-consuming pathways including glycogenesis, lipogenesis, and protein synthesis. It simultaneously enhances catabolic, ATP-generating processes. Of the options listed, only glycogenesis is an anabolic process suppressed by AMPK.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glycolysis is a catabolic, ATP-producing pathway that AMPK enhances.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-oxidation is catabolic and is promoted by AMPK to generate ATP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: AMPK promotes GLUT4 translocation to bring in glucose.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucose uptake is increased, not suppressed, by AMPK activation.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: AMPK monitors cellular energy status by responding to AMP. Although ATP hydrolysis primarily yields ADP, intracellular AMP rises sharply and serves as the sensitive low-energy signal. Which enzyme generates this amplified AMP signal?

explanation.summary: Adenylate kinase interconverts two ADP into ATP plus AMP, causing AMP to rise disproportionately during energy stress.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenylate kinase catalyzes 2 ADP into ATP + AMP. Because baseline AMP concentrations are very low, this reaction produces a large fold-change in AMP even when ATP and ADP change little. That amplified AMP signal is what AMPK and enzymes like PFK-1 and glycogen phosphorylase sense as low energy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: PFK-1 is a glycolytic enzyme that responds to AMP; it does not generate the AMP signal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glycogen phosphorylase is regulated by AMP but does not produce it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ATP synthase makes ATP from ADP and phosphate and does not generate AMP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Creatine kinase buffers ATP using phosphocreatine and does not create the AMP signal.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has been well controlled on a sulfonylurea for a year. She now reports episodes of weakness and dizziness and is found to be hypoglycemic during a visit; her symptoms resolve after glucose administration. She recently began a daily walking and cycling routine. Which change best explains her new hypoglycemic episodes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exercise-driven AMPK activation adds insulin-independent glucose uptake on top of sulfonylurea-stimulated insulin secretion

explanation.summary: Adding exercise-induced GLUT4 uptake to a drug that already raises insulin can push glucose too low.
explanation.whyCorrect: Her sulfonylurea continuously augments insulin secretion. Daily exercise activates AMPK and deposits GLUT4 on skeletal muscle, adding insulin-independent glucose clearance. The two effects together can drive blood glucose below normal, producing the hypoglycemic episodes that resolve with glucose.

whyWrongByChoice.B: More dietary glucose would tend to raise, not lower, blood glucose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alcohol can cause hypoglycemia via a high NADH/NAD+ ratio, so decreasing intake would reduce, not cause, hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A standard multivitamin does not sensitize tissues to insulin or cause hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reducing sodium affects blood pressure and volume, not glucose homeostasis.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A severely malnourished 22-year-old with anorexia nervosa is admitted and started on nutritional support. Shortly after carbohydrate is reintroduced, she develops profound hypophosphatemia. What is the mechanism of the falling serum phosphate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insulin-stimulated glycolysis drives phosphate from serum into cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucose causes renal wasting of phosphate in the urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Her diet lacks phosphate, so serum levels simply fall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphate binds administered glucose in the gut lumen

explanation.summary: Re-feeding raises insulin, restarting glycolysis and kinase signaling that pull phosphate into cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reintroducing glucose after starvation triggers insulin secretion and restarts anabolic, phosphate-dependent processes such as glycolysis and kinase-mediated signaling. Phosphate rushes intracellularly to support these reactions, dropping serum levels. Severe hypophosphatemia in re-feeding syndrome can cause seizures, coma, or death.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The fall is due to an intracellular shift, not renal wasting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Serum phosphate can appear low-normal in starvation, but the acute drop is driven by cellular uptake, not dietary lack.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phosphate does not chelate glucose in the gut to lower serum levels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thiamine is given to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and does not cause phosphaturia.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A liberated prisoner of war with prolonged starvation is refed with intravenous dextrose. Soon afterward he becomes confused with ophthalmoplegia and ataxia. Deficiency of which nutrient, whose demand surges when glucose is metabolized, best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Refeeding glucose without adequate thiamin precipitates Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome because thiamin is a cofactor for PDH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thiamin is a required cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) and other enzymes of glucose oxidation. When glucose is reintroduced in a thiamin-deficient patient, the surge in demand outstrips supply, precipitating Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia. This is a key re-feeding consideration.

whyWrongByChoice.B: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and subacute combined degeneration, not glucose-triggered encephalopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and is not precipitated by glucose refeeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy, unrelated to this acute neurologic picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin D deficiency affects calcium and bone metabolism, not thiamin-dependent glucose oxidation.

questions.topicName: Cell Signaling in Context: Fed and Fasting Metabolic State (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A committee is setting nutrient intake guidance for a newly recognized micronutrient. There is not enough scientific evidence to calculate a value that meets the requirement of 50% of healthy individuals, so they instead rely on observed and experimentally approximated intakes of healthy population groups. Which dietary reference intake value are they establishing?

explanation.summary: Adequate Intake (AI) is used when an EAR cannot be determined.
explanation.whyCorrect: AI is a value based on observed or experimentally determined approximations of nutrient intake by groups of healthy people. It is set in lieu of an RDA when there is insufficient evidence to calculate an EAR, exactly the scenario described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The EAR is a defined value meeting the requirement of 50% of healthy individuals; the committee could not calculate it here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The RDA is derived from the EAR and covers 97-98% of individuals, so it also cannot be set without an EAR.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The UL is the safety ceiling for adverse effects, not a value for planning adequate intake.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DRI is the umbrella term for the entire set of reference values, not the specific value being established.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man in a region where maize is the dietary staple presents with a symmetric, hyperpigmented, scaly rash over sun-exposed skin of the arms and a collar-like rash around the neck. He also reports diarrhea, and his family notes progressive confusion and irritability. Deficiency of which vitamin best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Pellagra from niacin (B3) deficiency causes dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Niacin deficiency produces pellagra, defined by the 4 D's: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and death if untreated. The photosensitive dermatitis in sun-exposed areas with the collar-like Casal necklace is classic, and it is historically common in corn-based diets.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thiamin deficiency causes beriberi with cardiomyopathy and peripheral neuropathy, not a photosensitive rash with the 4 D's.
whyWrongByChoice.C: B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia and neurologic symptoms, not pellagra.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Folate deficiency causes macrocytic anemia or birth defects, not the pellagra triad.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy with bleeding gums and pinpoint hemorrhages, not the Casal necklace.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child presents with a pellagra-like photosensitive rash and intermittent cerebellar ataxia. Laboratory studies show a marked increase in neutral amino acids, including tryptophan, in the urine, while plasma amino acid levels are low. The underlying defect involves a transporter in the intestine and kidney. Which condition most likely accounts for this patient's niacin deficiency?

explanation.summary: Hartnup disease impairs tryptophan absorption, reducing niacin availability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hartnup disease is a defective neutral amino acid transporter in the intestine and kidney. Tryptophan fails to be absorbed and is lost in the urine; because tryptophan can be catabolized to nicotinamide for NAD+, this decrement can culminate in pellagra.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carcinoid syndrome depletes tryptophan by shunting it into excess serotonin synthesis, not by a defective amino acid transporter with aminoaciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isoniazid depletes vitamin B6 needed to synthesize niacin, but it does not cause neutral aminoaciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Biotinidase deficiency impairs biotin recycling and causes a biotin, not niacin, deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PKU results from defective phenylalanine hydroxylase and does not cause tryptophan wasting or pellagra.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a long history of chronic alcohol use is admitted with dilated cardiomyopathy and high-output heart failure along with a symmetric peripheral sensory neuropathy. His symptoms improve rapidly after intravenous administration of a specific vitamin. This vitamin's active cofactor is required by transketolase and by pyruvate dehydrogenase. Which vitamin was administered?

explanation.summary: Thiamin (B1) deficiency causes beriberi and is common in chronic alcoholism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thiamin provides thiamin pyrophosphate (TPP), the cofactor for oxidative decarboxylation enzymes such as pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and for transketolase. Its deficiency causes beriberi with cardiac and peripheral nerve dysfunction and is especially likely in chronic alcoholism.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Riboflavin gives FMN/FAD; deficiency is linked to impaired iron absorption and possible normocytic anemia, not beriberi.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Niacin gives NAD+/NADP+; its deficiency causes pellagra, not high-output cardiac failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyridoxine gives PLP; deficiency causes convulsions and microcytic anemia, and its cofactor is not used by transketolase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biotin supports carboxylation reactions; deficiency causes dermatitis and metabolic acidosis, not beriberi.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man who eats an extremely limited diet with almost no fruits or vegetables presents with bleeding, swollen gums, perifollicular hemorrhages, and a poorly healing surgical wound. The deficient vitamin acts as a coenzyme for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase. Impaired activity of these enzymes most directly explains his findings through defective synthesis of which molecule?

explanation.summary: Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) impairs collagen hydroxylation and cross-linking.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin C is a coenzyme for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase, which hydroxylate proline and lysine residues so collagen can cross-link and form stable, mature protein. Without it, collagen is weak, producing the bleeding gums, pinpoint hemorrhages, and poor wound healing of scurvy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heme synthesis begins with ALA synthase using PLP and glycine, and is not the target of prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Serotonin synthesis requires BH4 and PLP, not vitamin C-dependent collagen hydroxylases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nicotinamide for NAD+ derives from tryptophan catabolism, unrelated to collagen hydroxylation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methionine is regenerated by methionine synthase using B12 and folate, not by collagen hydroxylases.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman has been taking more than 2 grams per day of a vitamin supplement for premenstrual symptoms for several months, roughly 20 times the tolerable upper intake level. She develops numbness beginning in the feet, then loss of sensation in the hands, and eventually difficulty walking. Symptoms resolve after she stops the supplement. Excess of which vitamin best explains this toxicity?

explanation.summary: Excess vitamin B6 causes a dose-dependent peripheral sensory neuropathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin B6 is one of the two water-soluble vitamins (with B3) that can cause adverse effects in excess. Toxicity was recognized in studies of megadose B6 for premenstrual syndrome, producing sensory neuropathy that starts as numb feet and can progress to inability to walk, reversing after supplements are stopped.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Excess niacin causes a prostaglandin-mediated flush, not a progressive sensory neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The vitamin C UL is 2000 mg/d and excess does not cause this ascending neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thiamin has no established UL and is considered safe even at high doses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pantothenate has no established RDA and deficiency, not excess, is essentially the only concern, and even that is very rare.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)

questions.stem: A bodybuilder who consumes several raw egg whites daily for months develops thinning hair, a scaly dry rash, and fatigue. His diet is otherwise adequate. A protein in the raw egg white tightly binds a vitamin required by carboxylase enzymes. Which vitamin is most likely deficient?

explanation.summary: Avidin in raw egg white binds biotin and can cause biotin deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Biotin is the cofactor for carboxylase enzymes such as pyruvate carboxylase. Raw eggs contain avidin, which tightly binds biotin; regular consumption of raw eggs in high quantities can deplete biotin, causing thinning hair and scaly, dry skin. Heat denatures avidin, so cooked eggs are not a concern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Riboflavin is not bound by avidin and its deficiency affects iron absorption and can cause anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate deficiency causes macrocytic anemia, and folate is not bound by avidin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia and neurologic disease, unrelated to raw egg intake.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyridoxine is not bound by avidin; its deficiency causes convulsions and microcytic anemia.

questions.topicName: Basic Nutritional Requirements for Metabolism (1 of 3)
